[[da:Sahara]] [[de:Sahara]] [[fr:Désert du Sahara]][[nl:Sahara]] [[it:Deserto Del Sahara]] [[ja:サハラ砂漠]]
The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], located in northern [[Africa]].  Its name is derived from the [[Arabic language|Arabic]] ''Sahhra'', meaning ''desert''.

The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].  These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct.  The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan]].

During the last [[Ice Age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place than it is today.  [[Petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive.  [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here.  The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountain ranges.

==See also==

* [[Sahara Desert (ecoregion)]] (PA1327) - [[ecoregion]]

==External links==
*[http://www.sahara-overland.com/routes/index.htm Trans-Sahara routes]

[[da:Sahara]] [[de:Sahara]] [[fr:Désert du Sahara]][[nl:Sahara]] [[it:Deserto Del Sahara]] [[ja:サハラ砂漠]] [[sv:Sahara]]
The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old.  Its name is derived from the [[Arabic language|Arabic]] ''Sahhra'', meaning ''desert''.

The boundaries of the Sahara are the [[Atlantic Ocean]] on the west, the [[Atlas Mountains]] and the [[Mediterranean Sea]] on the north, the [[Red Sea]] and [[Egypt]] on the east, and the [[Sudan]] and the valley of the [[Niger River]] on the south. Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti massif]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). 

The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].  These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct.  The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]].

Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[Ice Age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today.  [[Petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive.  [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here.  The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountain ranges has been this way since about 3000 c.e.

==History==

==See also==

* [[Sahara Desert (ecoregion)]] (PA1327) - [[ecoregion]]

[[da:Sahara]] [[de:Sahara]] [[fr:Désert du Sahara]][[nl:Sahara]] [[it:Deserto Del Sahara]] [[ja:サハラ砂漠]] [[sv:Sahara]]
The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], over 3,500,000 sq mi (9,065,000 sq km), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old.  The whole land area of [[United States of America]] fits inside it.  Its name is derived from the [[Arabic language|Arabic]] ''Sahhra'', meaning ''desert''.

The boundaries of the Sahara are the [[Atlantic Ocean]] on the west, the [[Atlas Mountains]] and the [[Mediterranean Sea]] on the north, the [[Red Sea]] and [[Egypt]] on the east, and the [[Sudan]] and the valley of the [[Niger River]] on the south. Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti massif]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). 

The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].  These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct.  The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]].

Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[Ice Age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today.  Over 30,000 [[Petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in Tissil Algeria.  [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here.  The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountain ranges and has been this way since about 3000 b.c.e.

==History==

'''Bubalus Period''', (35,000 - 8,000 BCE), remains show artistic stone engravings [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the buffalo (Bubalus antiquus), elephant, rhinoceros, and hippopotamus. This is mainly found in the southeaster area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men are armed with clubs, throwing sticks, axes and bows, but never spears.  The men are often wearing round helmets (10,000 b.c.e - 8,000 b.c.e).

'''Cattle Period''', (7,500 - 4,000 BCE)

'''Horse Period''', (3,000 - 2,000 BCE)

'''Camel Period''', (after 2,000 BCE)

''Imazighen Period'', (ca 1,200 BCE)

==See also==

* [[Sahara Desert (ecoregion)]] (PA1327) - [[ecoregion]]

==External links==
*[http://www.sahara-overland.com/routes/index.htm Trans-Sahara routes]

[[da:Sahara]] [[de:Sahara]] [[fr:Désert du Sahara]][[nl:Sahara]] [[it:Deserto Del Sahara]] [[ja:サハラ砂漠]] [[sv:Sahara]]
The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], over 3,500,000 sq mi (9,065,000 sq km), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old.  The whole land area of [[United States of America]] fits inside it.  Its name is derived from the [[Arabic language|Arabic]] ''Sahhra'', meaning ''desert''.

The boundaries of the Sahara are the [[Atlantic Ocean]] on the west, the [[Atlas Mountains]] and the [[Mediterranean Sea]] on the north, the [[Red Sea]] and [[Egypt]] on the east, and the [[Sudan]] and the valley of the [[Niger River]] on the south. Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti massif]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). 

The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].  These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct.  The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]].

Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[Ice Age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today.  Over 30,000 [[Petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in Tissil Algeria.  [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here.  The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountain ranges and has been this way since about 3000 b.c.e.

==Natural Resources ==

There are large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and phosphates in Morocco and Western Sahara.

==History==

'''Bubalus Period''', (35,000 - 8,000 BCE), remains show artistic stone engravings [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the buffalo (Bubalus antiquus), elephant, rhinoceros, and hippopotamus. This is mainly found in the southeaster area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men are armed with clubs, throwing sticks, axes and bows, but never spears.  The men are often wearing round helmets (10,000 b.c.e - 8,000 b.c.e).

'''Cattle Period''', (7,500 - 4,000 BCE), begining of a pastoral economy, domesticated cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of pottery making. Manufacture of polished stone axes, grindstones and arrowheads, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting.  The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle herding.  

'''Imazighen Period''', (3,000 - 700 BCE), 

'''Carthage''', (ca 700 BCE)

'''Rome''', (ca 200 BCE)

'''Vandals''' (ca 429 CE)

'''Roman Reconquest''', (ca 532 CE)

'''Arab Conquest''', (ca 647 CE)

'''European Conquest of Ottomans''', (ca 1500 CE)

The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], over 3,500,000 sq mi (9,065,000 sq km), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old. The whole land area of [[United States of America]] fits inside it. Its name is derived from the [[Arabic language|Arabic]] ''Sahhra'', meaning ''desert''. Which was derived from the Sumerian term 'sahar'. sahar: silt, dust, sand, earth, mud, loam; rubbish; sediment (cf., kuš7) (sa5, 'red-brown', + hara, 'crushed, pulverized').
[[Image:Sahara_land_area.gif|Map of Sahara]]
== Overview == The boundaries of the Sahara are the [[Atlantic Ocean]] on the west, the [[Atlas Mountains]] and the [[Mediterranean Sea]] on the north, the [[Red Sea]] and [[Egypt]] on the east, and the [[Sudan]] and the valley of the [[Niger River]] on the south. Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti massif]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]]. These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct. The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]]. Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[Ice Age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today. Over 30,000 [[Petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in Tissil Algeria. [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here. The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountain ranges and has been this way since about 3000 b.c.e. 2.5 million people live in Sahara, most of these in Mauritania, Morocco and Algeria. Dominant groups of people are Sahrawis, Tuareg and Negroids. The largest city is Nouakchott, Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are Tamanrasset, Algeria and Ghat, Libya. ==Natural Resources == There are large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and phosphates in Morocco and Western Sahara. ==History== '''Bubalus Period''', (35,000 - 8,000 BCE), remains show artistic stone engravings [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the buffalo (Bubalus antiquus), elephant, rhinoceros, and hippopotamus. This is mainly found in the southeaster area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men are armed with clubs, throwing sticks, axes and bows, but never spears. The men are often wearing round helmets (10,000 b.c.e - 8,000 b.c.e). '''Cattle Period''', (7,500 - 4,000 BCE), beginning of a pastoral economy, domesticated cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of pottery making. Manufacture of polished stone axes, grindstones and arrowheads, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting. Domesticated animals are Asian imports. The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle herding. '''Imazighen Period''', (3,000 - 700 BCE), The early period shows the importation of horses, camels and milking cows and large scale agriculture. The use and forging of Iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c 1220 BCE). They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to Egypt, generally settling on the coasts but settled in the desert also. '''Carthage''', (ca 700 BCE) '''Rome''', (ca 200 BCE) '''Vandals''' (ca 429 CE) '''Roman Reconquest''', (ca 532 CE) '''Arab Conquest''', (ca 647 CE) '''European Conquest of Ottomans''', (ca 1500 CE) ==See also== * [[Sahara Desert (ecoregion)]] (PA1327) - [[ecoregion]] ==External links== *[http://www.sahara-overland.com/routes/index.htm Trans-Sahara routes] ==References== *Michael Brett and Elizabeth Frentess. The Berbers. Blackwell Publishers. 1996. *Hugh Kennedy. Muslim Spain and Portugal: A Political History of al-Andalus. Longman, 1996. *Abdallah Laroui. The History of the Maghrib: An Interpretive Essay. Princeton, 1977. *Charles-Andre Julien. History of North Africa: From the Arab Conquest to 1830. The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], over 3,500,000 sq mi (9,065,000 sq km), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old. The whole land area of [[United States of America]] fits inside it. Its name ''Sahara'' صحر is the Arabic translation of the [[Tuareg]] word [[Tenere]] meaning ''desert''. The word Sahara was derived from the Sumerian term 'sahar'. sahar: silt, dust, sand, earth, mud, loam; rubbish; sediment (cf., kuš7) (sa5, 'red-brown', + hara, 'crushed, pulverized').

Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti massif]] the [[Ayr Mountains]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), [[Tenere]] desert and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). The highest peak in the Sahara is the Emi Koussi Mount in Tibesti massif in northern Chad. The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]]. These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct. The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]]. Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[ice age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today. Over 30,000 [[Petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in Tissil Algeria. [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here. The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountain ranges and has been this way since about 3000 b.c.e. 2.5 million people live in Sahara, most of these in Mauritania, Morocco and Algeria. Dominant groups of people are the Tuareg-Berber, the Sahrawis and Moors and different black African ethnicities including the Tubu, the Nubians, the Zaghawas and the Kanuri. The largest city is Nouakchott, Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are Tamanrasset, Algeria, Tin Buktu, Mali, Agadez, Niger, Ghat, Libya, and Faya in Chad. ==Natural Resources == There are large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of phosphates in Morocco and Western Sahara. ==History== '''Bubalus Period''', (35,000 - 8,000 BCE), remains show artistic stone engravings [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the buffalo (Bubalus antiquus), elephant, rhinoceros, and hippopotamus. This is mainly found in the southeaster area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men are armed with clubs, throwing sticks, axes and bows, but never spears. The men are often wearing round helmets (10,000 b.c.e - 8,000 b.c.e). '''Cattle Period''', (7,500 - 4,000 BCE), beginning of a pastoral economy, domesticated cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of pottery making. Manufacture of polished stone axes, grindstones and arrowheads, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting. Domesticated animals are Asian imports. The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle herding. '''Imazighen Period''', (3,000 - 700 BCE), The early period shows the importation of horses, camels and milking cows and large scale agriculture. The use and forging of Iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c 1220 BCE). They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to Egypt, generally settling on the coasts but settled in the desert also. The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], over 3,500,000 sq mi (9,065,000 sq km), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old.  The whole land area of [[United States of America]] fits inside it.  Its name ''Sahara'' صحر is the [[Arabic]] translation of the [[Tuareg]] word [[Tenere]] meaning ''desert''.

[[Image:Sahara_land_area.gif|right|Map of Sahara]]

Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti massif]] the [[Ayr Mountains]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), [[Tenere]] desert and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). The highest peak in the Sahara is the [[Emi Koussi Mount]] in [[Tibesti massif]] in northern [[Chad]].

The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].  These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct.  The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]].

Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[ice age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today.  Over 30,000 [[petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in [[Tissil]] [[Algeria]].  [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here.  The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountain ranges and has been this way since about 3000 b.c.e.

2.5 million people live in Sahara, most of these in [[Mauritania]], [[Morocco]] and [[Algeria]]. Dominant groups of people are the [[Tuareg]]-[[Berber]], the [[Sahrawis]], [[Moors]], and different black African ethnicities including the [[Tubu]], the [[Nubians]], the [[Zaghawas]] and the [[Kanuri]]. The largest city is [[Nouakchott]], Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are [[Tamanrasset]], Algeria, [[Tin Buktu]], [[Mali]], [[Agadez]], [[Niger]], [[Ghat]], [[Libya]], and [[Faya]], [[Chad]].

==Natural Resources ==

There are large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of [[phosphates]] in [[Morocco]] and [[Western Sahara]].

==History==

'''Bubalus Period''', (35,000 - 8,000 BCE), remains show artistic stone engravings [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the[[ buffalo]] (Bubalus antiquus), [[elephant]], [[rhinoceros]], and [[hippopotamus]]. This is mainly found in the southeastern area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men are armed with [[clubs]], throwing sticks, [[axe]]s and [[bow]]s, but never [[spear]]s.  The men are often wearing round [[helmet]]s (10,000 b.c.e - 8,000 b.c.e).

'''Cattle Period''', (7,500 - 4,000 BCE), beginning of a pastoral economy, [[domestication|domesticated]] cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of [[pottery]] making. Manufacture of polished stone axes, [[grindstone]]s and [[arrowhead]]s, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting.  Domesticated animals are Asian imports.  The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle [[herding]].

'''Imazighen Period''', (3,000 - 700 BCE),  The early period shows the importation of [[horse]]s, [[camel]]s and [[milking]] cows and large scale [[agriculture]].  The use and forging of iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c 1220 BCE).  They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to [[Egypt]], generally settling on the coasts but sometimes in the desert also.

The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], over 3,500,000 sq mi (9,065,000 sq km), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old.  The whole land area of [[United States|United States of America]] fits inside it.  Its name ''Sahara'' صحراء is the [[Arabic]] translation of the [[Tuareg]] word [[Tenere]] meaning ''desert''.

[[Image:Sahara_land_area.gif|right|Map of Sahara]]

==Overview==
The boundaries of the Sahara are the [[Atlantic Ocean]] on the west, the [[Atlas Mountains]] and the [[Mediterranean Sea]] on the north, the [[Red Sea]] and [[Egypt]] on the east, and the [[Sudan]] and the valley of the [[River Niger]] on the south. Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti massif]] the [[Ayr Mountains]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), [[Tenere]] desert and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). The highest peak in the Sahara is the [[Emi Koussi Mount]] in [[Tibesti massif]] in northern [[Chad]].

The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].  These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct and ass.  The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]].

Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[ice age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today.  Over 30,000 [[petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in [[Tissil]] [[Algeria]].  [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here.  The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountain ranges and has been this way since about 3000 BC.

2.5 million people live in Sahara, most of these in [[Mauritania]], [[Morocco]] and [[Algeria]]. Dominant groups of people are the [[Tuareg]]-[[Berber]], the [[Sahrawis]], [[Moors]], and different black African ethnicities including the [[Tubu]], the [[Nubians]], the [[Zaghawas]] and the [[Kanuri]]. The largest city is [[Nouakchott]], Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are [[Tamanrasset]], Algeria, [[Timbuktu]], [[Mali]], [[Agadez]], [[Niger]], [[Ghat]], [[Libya]], and [[Faya]], [[Chad]].

==History==

'''[[Bubalus Period]]''', (35,000–8,000 BCE), remains show artistic stone engravings [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the [[buffalo]] (Bubalus antiquus), [[elephant]], [[rhinoceros]], and [[hippopotamus]]. This is mainly found in the southeastern area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men are armed with [[clubs]], throwing sticks, [[axe]]s and [[Bow (weapon)|bow]]s, but never [[spear]]s.  The men are often wearing round [[helmet]]s (10,000–8,000 BC).

'''[[Cattle Period]]''', (7,500–4,000 BC), beginning of a pastoral economy, [[domestication|domesticated]] cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of [[pottery]] making. Manufacture of polished stone axes, [[grindstone]]s and [[arrowhead]]s, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting.  Domesticated animals are Asian imports.  The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle [[herding]].

'''[[Imazighen Period]]''', (3,000–700 BC),  The early period shows the importation of [[horse]]s, [[camel]]s and [[milking]] cows and large scale [[agriculture]].  The use and forging of iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c 1220 BC).  They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to [[Egypt]], generally settling on the coasts but sometimes in the desert also.

By 2500 BCE the Sahara was as dry as it is today and it became a largely impenetrable barrier to human, with only scattered settlements around the oasis, but little trade or commerce through the dessert.  The one major exception was the [[Nile Valley]].  This well watered section of the desert became one of the most densely populated regions on the planet and the home to one of humanity’s earliest civilizations.  The Nile, however, was impassable at several [[cataract]]s making trade and contact difficult.  Overtime Egypt spread south and  technologies such as [[iron]] working, and perhaps ideas such as that of [[monarchy]] spread into [[Nubia]] and further south.

By 500 BCE a new influence arrived in the form of the [[Ancient Greece|Greeks]] and [[Phoenicians]].  Greek traders spread along the eastern coast of the dessert, establishing trading colonies along the [[Red Sea]] coast.  The Carthaginians explored the Atlantic coast of the desert.  The turbulence of the waters and the lack of markets never led to an extensive presence further south than modern Morocco.  Centralized states thus surrounded the desert on the north and east, it remained outside of the control of these states.  Raids from the nomadic [[Berber]] people of the desert were a constant concern of those living on the edge of the desert. 

The greatest change in the history of the Sahara arrived with the [[Arab]] invasion that brought [[camel]]s to the region.  For the first time an efficient trade across the Sahara desert could be conducted.  The kingdoms of the [[Sahel]] grew rich and powerful exporting [[gold]] to [[North Africa]].  The emirates along the Mediterranean sent south manufactured goods and [[horse]]s.  From the Sahara itself salt was exported.  This process turned the scattered oasis communities into trading centres, and brought them under the control of the empires on the edge of the desert.

This trade persisted for several centuries until the development in Europe of the [[caravel]] allowed ships, first from [[Portugal]] but soon from all Western Europe, to sail around the desert and gather the resources from the source in [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]].  The Sahara was rapidly remarginalized.

The colonial powers also largely ignored to the region the modern era has seen a number of mines and communities develop to exploit the deserts natural resources.  These include large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of [[Phosphate|phosphates]] in [[Morocco]] and [[Western Sahara]].

The emirates along the Mediterranean sent south manufactured goods and [[horse]]s.  From the Sahara itself salt was exported.  This process turned the scattered oasis communities into trading centres, and brought them under the control of the empires on the edge of the desert.

This trade persisted for several centuries until the development in Europe of the [[caravel]] allowed ships, first from [[Portugal]] but soon from all Western Europe, to sail around the desert and gather the resources from the source in [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]]. [[simple:Sahara Desert]]

The '''Sahara''' is the world's largest [[desert]], over 3,500,000 sq mi (9,065,000 sq km), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old.  The entire land area of the [[United States|United States of America]] would fit inside it. Its name ''Sahara'' صحراء is the [[Arabic]] translation of the [[Tuareg]] word [[Tenere]] meaning ''desert''.

[[Image:Sahara_land_area.gif|right|Map of Sahara]]

==Overview==
The boundaries of the Sahara are the [[Atlantic Ocean]] on the west, the [[Atlas Mountains]] and the [[Mediterranean Sea]] on the north, the [[Red Sea]] and [[Egypt]] on the east, and the [[Sudan]] and the valley of the [[River Niger]] on the south. Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti massif]] the [[Aïr Mountains]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), [[Tenere]] desert and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). The highest peak in the Sahara is [[Emi Koussi]] (3415 m) in the [[Tibesti Mountains]] in northern [[Chad]].

The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]]. These two regions are culturally and climatically distinct. The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]].

Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[ice age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today. Over 30,000 [[petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in the [[Tassili n'Ajjer]] in southeast [[Algeria]]. [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here. The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountains and has been this way since about [[3rd millennium BC|3000 BC]].

2.5 million people live in Sahara, most of these in [[Mauritania]], [[Morocco]] and [[Algeria]]. Dominant groups of people are the [[Tuareg]]-[[Berber]], the [[Sahrawis]], [[Moors]], and different black African ethnicities including the [[Tubu]], the [[Nubians]], the [[Zaghawas]] and the [[Kanuri]]. The largest city is [[Nouakchott]], Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are [[Tamanrasset]], Algeria; [[Timbuktu]], [[Mali]]; [[Agadez]], [[Niger]]; [[Ghat]], [[Libya]]; and [[Faya]], [[Chad]].

==History==

'''[[Bubalus Period]]''', ([[35th millennium BC|35,000]] - [[9th millennium BC|8,000 BC]]), remains show artistic stone engravings [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the [[buffalo]] (Bubalus antiquus), [[elephant]], [[rhinoceros]], and [[hippopotamus]]. This is mainly found in the southeastern area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men are armed with [[clubs]], throwing sticks, [[axe]]s and [[Bow (weapon)|bow]]s, but never [[spear]]s. The men are often wearing round [[helmet]]s ([[10th millennium BC|10,000]] - [[9th millennium BC|8,000 BC]]).

'''[[Cattle Period]]''', ([[8th millennium BC|7,500]] - [[5th millennium BC|4,000 BC]]), beginning of a pastoral economy, [[domestication|domesticated]] cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of [[pottery]] making. Manufacture of polished stone axes, [[grindstone]]s and [[arrowhead]]s, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting. Domesticated animals are Asian imports.  The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle [[herding]].

'''[[Imazighen Period]]''', ([[30th century BC|3,000]] - [[700 BC]]), The early period shows the importation of [[horse]]s, [[camel]]s and [[milking]] cows and large scale [[agriculture]]. The use and forging of iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c. [[13th century BC|1220 BC]]). They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to [[Egypt]], generally settling on the coasts but sometimes in the desert also.

By [[25th century BC|2500 BC]] the Sahara was as dry as it is today and it became a largely impenetrable barrier to human, with only scattered settlements around the oasis, but little trade or commerce through the desert. The one major exception was the [[Nile Valley]]. This well watered section of the desert became one of the most densely populated regions on the planet and the home to one of humanity’s earliest civilizations.  The Nile, however, was impassable at several [[cataract]]s making trade and contact difficult. Over time Egypt spread south and technologies such as [[iron]] working, and perhaps ideas such as that of [[monarchy]] spread into [[Nubia]] and further south.

By [[500 BC]] a new influence arrived in the form of the [[Ancient Greece|Greeks]] and [[Phoenicians]]. Greek traders spread along the eastern coast of the dessert, establishing trading colonies along the [[Red Sea]] coast. The Carthaginians explored the Atlantic coast of the desert. The turbulence of the waters and the lack of markets never led to an extensive presence further south than modern Morocco. Centralized states thus surrounded the desert on the north and east, it remained outside of the control of these states. Raids from the nomadic [[Berber]] people of the desert were a constant concern of those living on the edge of the desert.

The greatest change in the history of the Sahara arrived with the [[Arab]] invasion that brought [[camel]]s to the region. For the first time an efficient trade across the Sahara desert could be conducted. The kingdoms of the [[Sahel]] grew rich and powerful exporting [[gold]] to [[North Africa]]. The emirates along the Mediterranean sent south manufactured goods and [[horse]]s.  From the Sahara itself salt was exported. This process turned the scattered oasis communities into trading centres, and brought them under the control of the empires on the edge of the desert.

This trade persisted for several centuries until the development in Europe of the [[caravel]] allowed ships, first from [[Portugal]] but soon from all Western Europe, to sail around the desert and gather the resources from the source in [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]]. The Sahara was rapidly remarginalized.

The colonial powers also largely ignored to the region the modern era has seen a number of mines and communities develop to exploit the deserts natural resources. These include large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of [[Phosphate|phosphates]] in [[Morocco]] and [[Western Sahara]].

Manufacturing of polished stone axes, [[grindstone]]s and [[arrowhead]]s, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting. Domesticated animals are Asian imports.  The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle [[herding]].
[[Image:ST-caravan.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A caravan in the Sahara]]
'''[[Imazighen Period]]''', ([[30th century BC|3,000]] - [[700 BC]]), The early period shows the importation of [[horse]]s, [[camel]]s and [[milking]] cows and large scale [[agriculture]]. The use and forging of iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c. [[13th century BC|1220 BC]]). They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to [[Egypt]], generally settling on the coasts but sometimes in the desert also.

By [[25th century BC|2500 BC]] the Sahara was as dry as it is today and it became a largely impenetrable barrier to humans, with only scattered settlements around the oasis, but little trade or commerce through the desert. The one major exception was the [[Nile Valley]]. This well watered section of the desert became one of the most densely populated regions on the planet and the home to one of humanity's earliest civilizations. The Nile, however, was impassable at several [[cataract]]s making trade and contact difficult. Over time Egypt spread south and technologies such as [[iron]] working, and perhaps ideas such as that of [[monarchy]] spread into [[Nubia]] and further south.

By [[500 BC]] a new influence arrived in the form of the [[Ancient Greece|Greeks]] and [[Phoenicians]]. Greek traders spread along the eastern coast of the desert, establishing trading colonies along the [[Red Sea]] coast. The Carthaginians explored the Atlantic coast of the desert. The turbulence of the waters and the lack of markets never led to an extensive presence further south than modern Morocco. Centralized states thus surrounded the desert on the north and east, it remained outside of the control of these states. Raids from the nomadic [[Berber]] people of the desert were a constant concern of those living on the edge of the desert.

The greatest change in the history of the Sahara arrived with the [[Arab]] invasion that brought [[camel]]s to the region. For the first time an efficient trade across the Sahara desert could be conducted. The kingdoms of the [[Sahel]] grew rich and powerful exporting [[gold]] to [[North Africa]]. The emirates along the Mediterranean sent south manufactured goods and [[horse]]s.  From the Sahara itself salt was exported. This process turned the scattered oasis communities into trading centres, and brought them under the control of the empires on the edge of the desert.

This trade persisted for several centuries until the development in Europe of the [[caravel]] allowed ships, first from [[Portugal]] but soon from all Western Europe, to sail around the desert and gather the resources from the source in [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]]. The Sahara was rapidly remarginalized.

The colonial powers also largely ignored the region, but the modern era has seen a number of mines and communities develop to exploit the desert's natural resources. These include large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of [[Phosphate|phosphates]] in [[Morocco]] and [[Western Sahara]].

[[Image:Sahara satellite.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Satellite image]]
The '''Sahara'''  is the world's second largest [[desert]] (second to [[Antarctica]]), over 9,000,000 km² (3,500,000 mi²), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old.  The entire land area of the [[United States|United States of America]] would fit inside it. Its name, ''Sahara'', (صحراء {{Audio|ar-Sahara.ogg|pronunciation}}) means ''desert'' in [[Arabic language|arabic]].

==Overview==
The boundaries of the Sahara are the [[Atlantic Ocean]] on the west, the [[Atlas Mountains]] and the [[Mediterranean Sea]] on the north, the [[Red Sea]] and [[Egypt]] on the east, and the [[Sudan]] and the valley of the [[River Niger]] on the south. Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti Mountains|Tibesti massif]] the [[Aïr Mountains]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), [[Tenere]] desert and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). The highest peak in the Sahara is [[Emi Koussi]] (3415 m) in the [[Tibesti Mountains]] in northern [[Chad]].
[[Image:Sahara desert.jpg|thumb|right|]]
The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].  The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]].

Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[ice age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today. Over 30,000 [[petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in the [[Tassili n'Ajjer]] in southeast [[Algeria]]. [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here. The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountains and has been this way since about [[3rd millennium BC|3000 BC]].

2.5 million people live in Sahara, most of these in [[Mauritania]], [[Morocco]] and [[Algeria]]. Dominant groups of people are the [[Tuareg]]-[[Berber]], the [[Sahrawis]], [[Moors]], and different black African ethnicities including the [[Tubu]], the [[Nubians]], the [[Zaghawas]] and the [[Kanuri]]. The largest city is [[Nouakchott]], Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are [[Tamanrasset]], Algeria; [[Timbuktu]], [[Mali]]; [[Agadez]], [[Niger]]; [[Ghat]], [[Libya]]; and [[Faya]], [[Chad]].

==History==
[[Morocco]] boasts the oldest [[figurine]] known to date: 300,000 to 500,000 years ago.

[[Algeria]] has provided evidence of remarkable workmanship in [[tool]]-making as early as 30,000 BC. According to some sources, [[prehistory|prehistoric]] Algeria was the site of the ''highest state of development'' of Middle [[Paleolithic]] flake-tool techniques. Early remnants of hominid occupation have been found in Ain el Hanech, near Saïda (ca. 200,000 B.C.). Later, [[Neandertal]] tool makers produced hand axes in the [[Levalloisian]] and [[Mousterian]] styles (ca. 43,000 B.C.) similar to those in the Levant. Tools of the era starting about 30,000 BC are called Aterian (after the site [[Bir el Ater]]), south of [[Annaba (city)|Annaba]] in the north-eastern corner of Algeria, and are marked by a high standard of workmanship, great variety, and specialization. See [[Prehistory of Central North Africa]].

'''[[Bubalus Period]]''', ([[35th millennium BC|35,000]] - [[9th millennium BC|8,000 BC]]), remains show artistic stone engravings [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the [[buffalo]] (Bubalus antiquus), [[elephant]], [[rhinoceros]], and [[hippopotamus]]. This is mainly found in the southeastern area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men are armed with [[clubs]], throwing sticks, [[axe]]s and [[Bow (weapon)|bow]]s, but never [[spear]]s. Circa 18,000 BC are indications of [[shipbuilding]] in the area. Artistic impressions dating to between [[10th millennium BC|10,000]] and [[9th millennium BC|8,000 BC]] depict Saharan men as wearing round [[helmet]]s akin to those depicted in [[Olmec]] statues.

'''[[Cattle Period]]''', ([[8th millennium BC|7,500]] - [[5th millennium BC|4,000 BC]]), beginning of a pastoral economy, [[domestication|domesticated]] cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of [[pottery]] making. Manufacturing of polished stone axes, [[grindstone]]s and [[arrowhead]]s, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting. Domesticated animals are Asian imports.  The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle [[herding]].
[[Image:ST-caravan.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A caravan in the Sahara]]
'''[[Imazighen Period]]''', ([[30th century BC|3,000]] - [[700 BC]]), The early period shows the importation of [[horse]]s, [[camel]]s and [[milking]] cows and large scale [[agriculture]]. The use and forging of iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c. [[13th century BC|1220 BC]]). They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to [[Egypt]], generally settling on the coasts but sometimes in the desert also.

By [[25th century BC|2500 BC]] the Sahara was as dry as it is today and it became a largely impenetrable barrier to humans, with only scattered settlements around the oasis, but little trade or commerce through the desert. The one major exception was the [[Nile Valley]]. This well watered section of the desert became one of the most densely populated regions on the planet and the home to one of humanity's earliest civilizations. The Nile, however, was impassable at several [[cataract]]s making trade and contact difficult. Over time Egypt spread south and technologies such as [[iron]] working, and perhaps ideas such as that of [[monarchy]] spread into [[Nubia]] and further south.

Sometime between 633 and 530 BC [[Hanno the Navigator]] either established or reinforced [[Phoenicia]]n colonies in the [[Western Sahara]], but all ancient remains have vanished with virtually no trace. See [[History of Western Sahara]].

By [[500 BC]] a new influence arrived in the form of the [[Ancient Greece|Greeks]] and [[Phoenicians]]. Greek traders spread along the eastern coast of the desert, establishing trading colonies along the [[Red Sea]] coast. The Carthaginians explored the Atlantic coast of the desert. The turbulence of the waters and the lack of markets never led to an extensive presence further south than modern Morocco. Centralized states thus surrounded the desert on the north and east, it remained outside of the control of these states. Raids from the nomadic [[Berber]] people of the desert were a constant concern of those living on the edge of the desert.

The greatest change in the history of the Sahara arrived with the [[Arab]] invasion that brought [[camel]]s to the region. For the first time an efficient trade across the Sahara desert could be conducted. The kingdoms of the [[Sahel]] grew rich and powerful exporting [[gold]] to [[North Africa]]. The emirates along the Mediterranean sent south manufactured goods and [[horse]]s.  From the Sahara itself salt was exported. This process turned the scattered oasis communities into trading centres, and brought them under the control of the empires on the edge of the desert.

This trade persisted for several centuries until the development in Europe of the [[caravel]] allowed ships, first from [[Portugal]] but soon from all Western Europe, to sail around the desert and gather the resources from the source in [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]]. The Sahara was rapidly remarginalized.

The colonial powers also largely ignored the region, but the modern era has seen a number of mines and communities develop to exploit the desert's natural resources. These include large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of [[Phosphate|phosphates]] in [[Morocco]] and [[Western Sahara]].

Modern DNA analysis (see [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/query.fcgi?cmd=Retrieve&db=PubMed&dopt=Abstract&list_uids=11393336 Z. Brakez et al., "Human mitochondrial DNA sequence variation in the Moroccan population of the Souss area" extract]) has confirmed that various populations have contributed to the present-day gene pool of Morocco, including [[Berber]]s, [[Arab]]s, [[Phoenicia]]ns, [[Sephardic]] [[Jew]]s, and sub-Saharan Africans.

'''[[Cattle Period]]''', ([[8th millennium BC|7,500]] - [[5th millennium BC|4,000 BC]]), beginning of a pastoral economy, [[domestication|domesticated]] cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of [[pottery]] making. Manufacturing of polished stone axes, [[grindstone]]s and [[arrowhead]]s, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting. Domesticated animals are Asian imports.  The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle [[herding]].
[[Image:ST-caravan.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A caravan in the Sahara]]
'''[[Berber Period]]''', ([[30th century BC|3,000]] - [[700 BC]]), The early period shows the importation of [[horse]]s, [[camel]]s and [[milking]] cows and large scale [[agriculture]]. The use and forging of iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c. [[13th century BC|1220 BC]]). They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to [[Egypt]], generally settling on the coasts but sometimes in the desert also.

By [[25th century BC|2500 BC]] the Sahara was as dry as it is today and it became a largely impenetrable barrier to humans, with only scattered settlements around the oases, but little trade or commerce through the desert. The one major exception was the [[Nile Valley]]. This well watered section of the desert became one of the most densely populated regions on the planet and the home to one of humanity's earliest civilizations. The Nile, however, was impassable at several [[cataract]]s making trade and contact difficult. Over time Egypt spread south and technologies such as [[iron]] working, and perhaps ideas such as that of [[monarchy]] spread into [[Nubia]] and further south.

Sometime between 633 and 530 BC [[Hanno the Navigator]] either established or reinforced [[Phoenicia]]n colonies in the [[Western Sahara]], but all ancient remains have vanished with virtually no trace. See [[History of Western Sahara]].

By [[500 BC]] a new influence arrived in the form of the [[Ancient Greece|Greeks]] and [[Phoenicians]]. Greek traders spread along the eastern coast of the desert, establishing trading colonies along the [[Red Sea]] coast. The Carthaginians explored the Atlantic coast of the desert. The turbulence of the waters and the lack of markets never led to an extensive presence further south than modern Morocco. Centralized states thus surrounded the desert on the north and east; it remained outside of the control of these states. Raids from the nomadic [[Berber]] people of the desert were a constant concern of those living on the edge of the desert.

The greatest change in the history of the Sahara arrived with the [[Arab]] invasion that brought [[camel]]s to the region. For the first time an efficient trade across the Sahara desert could be conducted. The kingdoms of the [[Sahel]] grew rich and powerful exporting [[gold]] to [[North Africa]]. The emirates along the Mediterranean sent south manufactured goods and [[horse]]s.  From the Sahara itself salt was exported. This process turned the scattered oasis communities into trading centres, and brought them under the control of the empires on the edge of the desert.

This trade persisted for several centuries until the development in Europe of the [[caravel]] allowed ships, first from [[Portugal]] but soon from all Western Europe, to sail around the desert and gather the resources from the source in [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]]. The Sahara was rapidly remarginalized.

The colonial powers also largely ignored the region, but the modern era has seen a number of mines and communities develop to exploit the desert's natural resources. These include large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of [[Phosphate|phosphates]] in [[Morocco]] and [[Western Sahara]].

mtDNA analyses (see [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/query.fcgi?cmd=Retrieve&db=PubMed&dopt=Abstract&list_uids=11393336 Z. Brakez et al., "Human mitochondrial DNA sequence variation in the Moroccan population of the Souss area" extract]) found that various populations have contributed to the present-day gene pool of the [[Souss]] region of southern Morocco, including [[Berber]]s, [[Arab]]s, [[Phoenicia]]ns, [[Sephardic]] [[Jew]]s, and sub-Saharan Africans.  Throughout the Sahara, Berbers, Arabs, and sub-Saharan Africans are significantly represented genetically.

[[Image:Sahara satellite.jpg|thumb|right|300px|Satellite image]]
The '''Sahara'''  is the world's largest [[desert]], over 9,000,000 km² (3,500,000 mi²), located in northern [[Africa]] and is 2.5 million years old.  The entire land area of the [[United States|United States of America]] would fit inside it. Its name, ''Sahara'', is an English pronuciation of the word for ''desert'' in [[Arabic language|Arabic]] (صحراء {{Audio|ar-Sahara.ogg|arabic pronunciation}}).

==Overview==
The boundaries of the Sahara are the [[Atlantic Ocean]] on the west, the [[Atlas Mountains]] and the [[Mediterranean Sea]] on the north, the [[Red Sea]] and [[Egypt]] on the east, and the [[Sudan]] and the valley of the [[River Niger]] on the south. Sahara is divided into western Sahara, the central [[Ahaggar Mountains]], the [[Tibesti Mountains|Tibesti massif]], the [[Aïr Mountains]] (a region of desert mountains and high plateaus), [[Tenere]] desert and the [[Libyan desert]] (the most arid region). The highest peak in the Sahara is [[Emi Koussi]] (3415 m) in the [[Tibesti Mountains]] in northern [[Chad]].
[[Image:Sahara desert.jpg|thumb|right|]]
The Sahara divides the [[continent]] into [[North Africa|North]] and [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].  The southern border of the Sahara is marked by a band of semiarid [[savanna]] called the [[Sahel]]; south of the Sahel lies the lusher [[Sudan (region)|Sudan]].

Humans have lived on the edge of the desert for almost 500,000 years. During the last [[ice age]], the Sahara was a much wetter place, much like [[East Africa]], than it is today. Over 30,000 [[petroglyph]]s of river animals such as [[crocodile]]s survive in total with half found in the [[Tassili n'Ajjer]] in southeast [[Algeria]]. [[Fossil]]s of [[dinosaur]]s have also been found here. The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountains and has been this way since about [[3rd millennium BC|3000 BC]].

2.5 million people live in the Sahara, most of these in [[Mauritania]], [[Morocco]] and [[Algeria]]. Dominant groups of people are the [[Tuareg]]-[[Berber]], the [[Sahrawis]], [[Moors]], and different black African ethnicities including the [[Tubu]], the [[Nubians]], the [[Zaghawas]] and the [[Kanuri]]. The largest city is [[Nouakchott]], Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are [[Tamanrasset]], Algeria; [[Timbuktu]], [[Mali]]; [[Agadez]], [[Niger]]; [[Ghat]], [[Libya]]; and [[Faya]], [[Chad]].

==History==
[[Morocco]] boasts the oldest [[figurine]] known to date: 300,000 to 500,000 years ago.

According to some sources, [[prehistory|prehistoric]] Algeria was the site of the ''highest state of development'' of Middle [[Paleolithic]] flake-tool techniques. Early remnants of hominid occupation have been found in Ain el Hanech, near Saïda (ca. 200,000 B.C.). Later, [[Neandertal]] tool makers produced hand axes in the [[Levalloisian]] and [[Mousterian]] styles (ca. 43,000 B.C.) similar to those in the Levant. Tools of the era starting about 30,000 BC are called Aterian (after the site [[Bir el Ater]]), south of [[Annaba (city)|Annaba]] in the north-eastern corner of Algeria, and are marked by a high standard of workmanship, great variety, and specialization. See [[Prehistory of Central North Africa]].

'''[[Bubalus Period]]''', ([[35th millennium BC|35,000]] - [[9th millennium BC|8,000 BC]]), remains show artistic stone engravings, [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the [[buffalo]] (''Bubalus antiquus''), [[elephant]], [[rhinoceros]], and [[hippopotamus]]. Dominant groups of people are the [[Tuareg]]-[[Berber]], the [[Sahrawis]], [[Moors]], and different black African ethnicities including the [[Tubu]], the [[Nubians]], the [[Zaghawa]]s and the [[Kanuri]]. The largest city is [[Nouakchott]], Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are [[Tamanrasset]], Algeria; [[Timbuktu]], [[Mali]]; [[Agadez]], [[Niger]]; [[Ghat]], [[Libya]]; and [[Faya]], [[Chad]].

==History==
[[Morocco]] boasts the oldest [[figurine]] known to date: 300,000 to 500,000 years ago.

[[Algeria]] has provided evidence of remarkable workmanship in [[tool]]-making as early as 30,000 BC. According to some sources, [[prehistory|prehistoric]] Algeria was the site of the ''highest state of development'' of Middle [[Paleolithic]] flake-tool techniques. Early remnants of hominid occupation have been found in Ain el Hanech, near Saïda (ca. 200,000 B.C.). Later, [[Neandertal]] tool makers produced hand axes in the [[Levalloisian]] and [[Mousterian]] styles (ca. 43,000 B.C.) similar to those in the Levant. Tools of the era starting about 30,000 BC are called Aterian (after the site [[Bir el Ater]]), south of [[Annaba (city)|Annaba]] in the north-eastern corner of Algeria, and are marked by a high standard of workmanship, great variety, and specialization. See [[Prehistory of Central North Africa]].

'''[[Bubalus Period]]''', ([[35th millennium BC|35,000]] - [[9th millennium BC|8,000 BC]]), remains show artistic stone engravings, [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the [[buffalo]] (''Bubalus antiquus''), [[elephant]], [[rhinoceros]], and [[hippopotamus]]. This is mainly found in the southeastern area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men often wearing round helmets ([[10000 BC]] - [[8000 BC]]) are armed with [[clubs]], throwing sticks, [[axe]]s and [[Bow (weapon)|bow]]s, but never [[spear]]s. A [[cereal|grain]]-[[grinding]] [[culture]] [[farming]] along the [[Nile]] around [[10th millennium BC|9500 BC]] used the earliest known type of [[sickle]] [[blade]].

'''[[Cattle Period]]''', ([[8th millennium BC|7,500]] - [[5th millennium BC|4,000 BC]]), beginning of a pastoral economy, [[domestication|domesticated]] cattle, sheep and goats, and the discovery of [[pottery]] making. Manufacturing of polished stone axes, [[Grindstone (tool)|grindstone]]s and [[arrowhead]]s, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting. Domesticated animals are Asian imports.  The later era shows the origins of villages supporting large populations and cattle [[herding]].
[[Image:ST-caravan.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A caravan in the Sahara]]
'''[[Berber Period]]''', ([[30th century BC|3,000]] - [[700 BC]]), The early period shows the importation of [[horse]]s, [[camel]]s and [[milking]] cows and large scale [[agriculture]]. The use and forging of iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c. [[13th century BC|1220 BC]]). They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to [[Egypt]], generally settling on the coasts but sometimes in the desert also.

By [[25th century BC|2500 BC]] the Sahara was as dry as it is today and it became a largely impenetrable barrier to humans, with only scattered [[settlement]]s around the oases, but little trade or commerce through the desert. The one major exception was the [[Nile|Nile Valley]]. This well watered section of the desert became one of the most densely populated regions on the planet and the home to one of humanity's earliest [[civilization]]s. The Nile, however, was impassable at several [[cataract]]s making trade and contact difficult. Over time Egypt spread south and technologies such as [[iron]] working, and perhaps ideas such as that of [[monarchy]] spread into [[Nubia]] and further south.

Sometime between 633 and 530 BC [[Hanno the Navigator]] either established or reinforced [[Phoenicia]]n colonies in the [[Western Sahara]], but all ancient remains have vanished with virtually no trace. See [[History of Western Sahara]].

By [[500 BC]] a new influence arrived in the form of the [[Ancient Greece|Greeks]] and [[Phoenicians]]. Greek traders spread along the eastern coast of the desert, establishing trading colonies along the [[Red Sea]] coast. The Carthaginians explored the Atlantic coast of the desert. The turbulence of the waters and the lack of markets never led to an extensive presence further south than modern Morocco. Centralized states thus surrounded the desert on the north and east; it remained outside of the control of these states. Raids from the nomadic [[Berber]] people of the desert were a constant concern of those living on the edge of the desert.

The greatest change in the history of the Sahara arrived with the [[Arab]] invasion that brought [[camel]]s to the region. For the first time an efficient trade across the Sahara desert could be conducted. The kingdoms of the [[Sahel]] grew rich and powerful exporting [[gold]] to [[North Africa]]. The emirates along the Mediterranean sent south manufactured goods and [[horse]]s.  From the Sahara itself salt was exported. This process turned the scattered oasis communities into trading centres, and brought them under the control of the empires on the edge of the desert.

This trade persisted for several centuries until the development in Europe of the [[caravel]] allowed ships, first from [[Portugal]] but soon from all Western Europe, to sail around the desert and gather the resources from the source in [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]]. The Sahara was rapidly remarginalized.

The colonial powers also largely ignored the region, but the modern era has seen a number of mines and communities develop to exploit the desert's natural resources. These include large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of [[Phosphate|phosphates]] in [[Morocco]] and [[Western Sahara]].

mtDNA analyses (see [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/query.fcgi?cmd=Retrieve&db=PubMed&dopt=Abstract&list_uids=11393336 Z. Brakez et al., "Human mitochondrial DNA sequence variation in the Moroccan population of the Souss area" extract]) found that various populations have contributed to the present-day gene pool of the [[Souss]] region of southern Morocco, including [[Berber]]s, [[Arab]]s, [[Phoenicia]]ns, [[Sephardic]] [[Jew]]s, and sub-Saharan Africans.  Throughout the Sahara, Berbers, Arabs, and sub-Saharan Africans are significantly represented genetically.

[[Image:Sahara desert.jpg|thumb|right|]]

The modern Sahara, though, is generally devoid of vegetation, except in the [[Nile]] Valley and at a few [[oasis|oases]] and in some scattered mountains and has been this way since about [[3rd millennium BC|3000 BC]].

Dominant groups of people are the [[Tuareg]]-[[Berber]], the [[Sahrawis]], [[Moors]], and different black African ethnicities including the [[Tubu]], the [[Nubians]], the [[Zaghawa]]s and the [[Kanuri]]. The largest city is [[Nouakchott]], Mauritania's capital. Other important cities are [[Tamanrasset]], Algeria; [[Timbuktu]], [[Mali]]; [[Agadez]], [[Niger]]; [[Ghat]], [[Libya]]; and [[Faya]], [[Chad]].

==History==
[[Morocco]] boasts the oldest [[figurine]] known to date: 300,000 to 500,000 years ago.

[[Algeria]] has provided evidence of remarkable workmanship in [[tool]]-making as early as 30,000 BC. According to some sources, [[prehistory|prehistoric]] Algeria was the site of the ''highest state of development'' of Middle [[Paleolithic]] flake-tool techniques. Early remnants of hominid occupation have been found in Ain el Hanech, near Saïda (ca. 200,000 B.C.). Later, [[Neandertal]] tool makers produced hand axes in the [[Levalloisian]] and [[Mousterian]] styles (ca. 43,000 B.C.) similar to those in the Levant. Tools of the era starting about 30,000 BC are called Aterian (after the site [[Bir el Ater]]), south of [[Annaba (city)|Annaba]] in the north-eastern corner of Algeria, and are marked by a high standard of workmanship, great variety, and specialization. See [[Prehistory of Central North Africa]].

===Bubalus Period===
[[Image:ST-caravan.jpg|thumb|right|250px|A caravan in the Sahara]]
([[35th millennium BC|35,000]] - [[9th millennium BC|8,000 BC]]): Remains show artistic stone engravings, [[petroglyphs]] and [[pictographs]] made of pigment mixed with milk of animals that became extinct in the area, including the [[buffalo]] (''Bubalus antiquus''), [[elephant]], [[rhinoceros]], and [[hippopotamus]]. This is mainly found in the southeastern area of modern Algeria, Chad and Libya. Men often wearing round helmets ([[10000 BC]] - [[8000 BC]]) are armed with [[clubs]], throwing sticks, [[axe]]s and [[Bow (weapon)|bow]]s, but never [[spear]]s. A [[cereal|grain]]-[[grinding]] [[culture]] [[farming]] along the [[Nile]] around [[10th millennium BC|9500 BC]] used the earliest known type of [[sickle]] [[blade]].

Archaeological findings indicate occupation in [[Nabta Playa]], located in the [[Nubian Desert]] of southern [[Egypt]], dating to somewhere between the [[10th millennium BC|10th]] and [[8th millennium BC|8th millennia BC]] [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html]. Evidence also indicates human habitation in the southwestern corner of Egypt, near the [[Sudan]] border, before [[8000 BC]]. Climate changes and/or overgrazing around [[8th millennium BC|8000 BC]] began to desiccate the pastoral lands of [[Ancient Egypt|Egypt]], eventually forming the Saharan desert, and early tribes naturally migrated to the [[Nile]] river where they developed a settled [[agriculture|agricultural]] [[economy]] and more centralized [[society]].

[[Archaeological]] finds also show that the [[Nabta Playa|Nabta]] peoples, between the [[10th millennium BC|10th]] and [[8th millennium BC|8th millennia BC]], were [[herding]] [[Domestication|domesticated]] [[cattle]] and using [[ceramic]]s adorned by complicated [[Paint|painted]] patterns created perhaps by using [[comb]]s [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html].

A [[pottery]] fragment of a large bowl that was discovered in [[Bir Kiseiba]] in southern Egypt has been dated to between [[8000 BC]] and [[7000 BC]] [http://smu.edu/newsinfo/releases/01033.html].

===Cattle Period===
([[8th millennium BC|7,500]] - [[5th millennium BC|4,000 BC]]): This period marks the onset of an extensive [[Pastoralism|pastoral]] economy: [[domestication|domesticated]] cattle, sheep and goats. Domesticated animals are [[Asian]] imports. [[Pottery]] making becomes more prevalent, as well as manufacturing of polished stone axes, [[Grindstone (tool)|grindstone]]s and [[arrowhead]]s, and the predominant use of bow and arrows for hunting. There is also evidence of [[cereal]] cultivation in the East Sahara in the [[7th millennium BC]].

Villages support large populations and cattle [[herding]]. By the [[7th millennium BC]] exceedingly large and organized [[settlement]]s are found in [[Nabta Playa]], along with deep [[well]]s that could supply water year-round [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html] and [[hut]]s lined up in straight [[row]]s [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html]. Sustenance in [[Nabta Playa]] in this era depended upon [[cattle]] [[herding]], [[fruit]], [[legume]]s, [[millet]]s, [[sorghum]] and [[tuber]]s [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html], but evidence shows only seasonal occupation of the [[Nabta Playa]] region. 

[[Goat]]s and [[sheep]] also appear in [[Nabta Playa]] in the late [[7th millennium BC]], apparently having also been imported from [[Southwest Asia]] [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html]. In this era, numerous large [[hearth]]s also are found in [[Nabta Playa]] [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html].

Interestingly, the domestication of the [[pig]] (see [http://www.touregypt.net/featurestories/pigs.htm]) in the Sahara and [[Ancient Egypt|ancient Egypt]] has been cited as a likely primary contributor to the [[desertification]] of the Sahara (see [[Sahara Desert (ecoregion)#Settings|Sahara Desert (ecoregion)]]).

By [[6000 BC]] predynastic Egyptians in the southwestern corner of [[Egypt]] were [[herding]] cattle and [[construction|constructing]] large buildings. Subsistence in organized and permanent [[settlement]]s in [[Predynastic Egypt|predynastic Egypt]] by the middle of the [[6th millennium BC]] centered predominantly on [[cereal]] and animal [[agriculture]]: [[cattle]], [[goat]]s, [[pig]]s and [[sheep]] [http://www.touregypt.net/ebph5.htm]. [[Metal]] objects replaced prior ones of [[stone]] [http://www.touregypt.net/ebph5.htm]. [[Tanning]] animal skins, [[pottery]] and [[weaving]] are commonplace in this era also [http://www.touregypt.net/ebph5.htm]. There are indications of seasonal or only temporary occupation of the [[Al Fayyum]] in the [[6th millennium BC]], with food activities centering on fishing, hunting and food-gathering [http://www.digitalegypt.ucl.ac.uk/fayum/fayumb.html]. Stone [[arrowhead]]s, [[knive]]s and [[scraper]]s are common [http://www.digitalegypt.ucl.ac.uk/fayum/fayumb.html]. [[Burial]] items include pottery, [[jewelry]], [[farming]] and [[hunting]] equipment, and assorted foods including dried meat and fruit [http://www.touregypt.net/ebph5.htm]. The dead are buried facing due west [http://www.touregypt.net/ebph5.htm].

By the [[6th millennium BC]], evidence of a prehistoric [[religion]] or [[cult]] appears in [[Nabta Playa]], with a number of [[sacrifice]]d cattle buried in [[stone]]-roofed chambers lined with [[clay]] [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html]. Other subterranean complexes are also found, including evidence of an early attempt at [[sculpture]] [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html].

====World's earliest known astronomy====
[[Image:Nabta.gif|thumb|125px|right|[[Nabta Playa|Nabta]] [[megalith]]]]
By the [[5th millennium BC]] the peoples of [[Nabta Playa]] had fashioned the world's earliest known [[Megalith|megalithic]] [[Astronomy|astronomical]] device, 1000 years older than but comparable to [[Stonehenge]]. Research shows it to be a prehistoric [[calendar]] that accurately marks the [[summer]] [[solstice]] [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html].

[[Archaeological]] discoveries reveal that the Nabta Playa peoples led livelihoods seemingly at a higher level of organization than their contemporaries who lived closer to the [[Nile|Nile Valley]] [http://www.comp-archaeology.org/WendorfSAA98.html]:
*above-ground & below-ground [[stone]] chamber [[construction]],
*[[village]]s designed in pre-planned arrangements,
*deep [[well]]s that held water year-round.

Analysis of human remains in [[Nabta Playa]] provide evidence of migration from [[Sub-Saharan Africa|sub-saharan Africa]] [http://hej3.as.utexas.edu/~www/wheel/africa/nabta_01.htm].

===Berber Period===
([[30th century BC|3,000]] - [[700 BC]]): The early period shows the importation of [[horse]]s, [[camel]]s and [[milking]] cows and large scale [[agriculture]]. The use and forging of iron came about from trade with the [[Phoenicians]] (c. [[13th century BC|1220 BC]]). They created a confederation of kingdoms across the entire Sahara to [[Egypt]], generally settling on the coasts but sometimes in the desert also.

By [[25th century BC|2500 BC]] the Sahara was as dry as it is today and it became a largely impenetrable barrier to humans, with only scattered [[settlement]]s around the oases, but little trade or commerce through the desert. The one major exception was the [[Nile|Nile Valley]]. This well watered section of the desert became one of the most densely populated regions on the planet and the home to one of humanity's earliest [[civilization]]s. The Nile, however, was impassable at several [[cataract]]s making trade and contact difficult. Over time Egypt spread south and technologies such as [[iron]] working, and perhaps ideas such as that of [[monarchy]] spread into [[Nubia]] and further south.

Sometime between 633 and 530 BC [[Hanno the Navigator]] either established or reinforced [[Phoenicia]]n colonies in the [[Western Sahara]], but all ancient remains have vanished with virtually no trace. See [[History of Western Sahara]].

By [[500 BC]] a new influence arrived in the form of the [[Ancient Greece|Greeks]] and [[Phoenicians]]. Greek traders spread along the eastern coast of the desert, establishing trading colonies along the [[Red Sea]] coast. The Carthaginians explored the Atlantic coast of the desert. The turbulence of the waters and the lack of markets never led to an extensive presence further south than modern Morocco. Centralized states thus surrounded the desert on the north and east; it remained outside of the control of these states. Raids from the nomadic [[Berber]] people of the desert were a constant concern of those living on the edge of the desert.

The greatest change in the history of the Sahara arrived with the [[Arab]] invasion that brought [[camel]]s to the region. For the first time an efficient trade across the Sahara desert could be conducted. The kingdoms of the [[Sahel]] grew rich and powerful exporting [[gold]] to [[North Africa]]. The emirates along the Mediterranean sent south manufactured goods and [[horse]]s.  From the Sahara itself salt was exported. This process turned the scattered oasis communities into trading centres, and brought them under the control of the empires on the edge of the desert.

This trade persisted for several centuries until the development in Europe of the [[caravel]] allowed ships, first from [[Portugal]] but soon from all Western Europe, to sail around the desert and gather the resources from the source in [[Guinea (region)|Guinea]]. The Sahara was rapidly remarginalized.

The colonial powers also largely ignored the region, but the modern era has seen a number of mines and communities develop to exploit the desert's natural resources. These include large deposits of oil and gas in Algeria and Libya and large deposits of [[Phosphate|phosphates]] in [[Morocco]] and [[Western Sahara]].

mtDNA analyses (see [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/query.fcgi?cmd=Retrieve&db=PubMed&dopt=Abstract&list_uids=11393336 Z. Brakez et al., "Human mitochondrial DNA sequence variation in the Moroccan population of the Souss area" extract]) found that various populations have contributed to the present-day gene pool of the [[Souss]] region of southern Morocco, including [[Berber]]s, [[Arab]]s, [[Phoenicia]]ns, [[Sephardic]] [[Jew]]s, and sub-Saharan Africans.  Throughout the Sahara, Berbers, Arabs, and sub-Saharan Africans are significantly represented genetically.
